sections.content:
The Atom: Foundation of Chemistry

All matter, including every molecule in a living cell, is made of atoms. An atom consists of a nucleus containing positively charged protons and neutral neutrons, surrounded by negatively charged electrons arranged in shells.

The number of protons defines the element (carbon always has 6 protons). The outermost shell — the valence shell — determines how an atom bonds to others. Carbon's valence shell holds 4 of its 8 possible electrons, so carbon always forms 4 bonds. This tetravalency is why carbon is the backbone of all organic molecules.

🔑 Key atoms in biology: Carbon (4 bonds), Hydrogen (1 bond), Oxygen (2 bonds), Nitrogen (3 bonds), Phosphorus (5 bonds), Sulfur (2 or 6 bonds). The mnemonic CHONPS covers the six most abundant elements in life.

Types of Chemical Bonds

Covalent Bonds

When two atoms share electrons, they form a covalent bond. These are strong bonds (150–400 kJ/mol) that define the structure of molecules. In a polar covalent bond, electrons are shared unequally — oxygen and nitrogen pull electrons toward themselves, creating partial charges (δ+ and δ−). This polarity is crucial for the behaviour of water and all biomolecules.

Ionic Bonds

When one atom transfers an electron to another, both gain stable configurations and the resulting opposite charges attract: an ionic bond. Important in protein structure and enzyme active sites, but relatively weak in aqueous environments.

Hydrogen Bonds

A hydrogen bond forms when a hydrogen already covalently bonded to an electronegative atom (O or N) is attracted to another electronegative atom. Individually weak (~20 kJ/mol), but collectively hydrogen bonds give water its remarkable properties and hold the two strands of the DNA double helix together.

Electronegativity & Polarity

Electronegativity increases across a period (left→right) and up a group in the periodic table. Oxygen (3.44) and nitrogen (3.04) are far more electronegative than carbon (2.55) or hydrogen (2.20). This difference drives polarity in C–O, C–N, and O–H bonds — polarity that biological systems exploit for recognition, catalysis, and information storage.

  • Non-polar bonds: C–H, C–C (ΔEN < 0.5)
  • Polar covalent: C–O, C–N, O–H (ΔEN 0.5–1.9)
  • Ionic: Na–Cl (ΔEN > 1.9)

Why This Matters for Biology

The combination of carbon's tetravalency, the polarity of C–O and C–N bonds, and the ability of hydrogen bonds to form and break quickly creates the chemical vocabulary of life. DNA stores information in hydrogen bonds between base pairs. Proteins fold because of hydrophobic collapse and hydrogen bonding. Enzymes catalyse reactions by manipulating bond polarity. Everything downstream in biochemistry and molecular biology builds on these atomic foundations.

questions.question: How many covalent bonds does carbon typically form?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 4 valence electrons and needs 4 more to complete its octet, so it always forms 4 covalent bonds — making it ideal as the backbone of complex biological molecules.

questions.question: Which type of bond holds the two strands of the DNA double helix together?
questions.explanation: Hydrogen bonds between complementary base pairs (A–T and G–C) hold the two DNA strands together. They are weak individually but collectively provide stable yet reversible base pairing.

questions.question: The mnemonic CHONPS refers to the six most abundant elements in living organisms. What does the 'P' stand for?
questions.explanation: Phosphorus is essential in nucleotides (DNA, RNA, ATP), phospholipid membranes, and cell signalling via phosphorylation.

questions.question: A C–O bond is described as polar covalent because:
options: Carbon and oxygen have the same electronegativity
Oxygen is more electronegative than carbon, pulling electron density toward itself
The electrons are transferred completely from carbon to oxygen
The bond involves sharing of protons, not electrons
questions.explanation: Oxygen (EN 3.44) is significantly more electronegative than carbon (EN 2.55), so it attracts the shared electrons more strongly, creating a partial negative charge on O and partial positive on C.

questions.question: Which property of hydrogen bonds makes them particularly important in biological systems?
options: They are the strongest bonds in biology
They are weak enough to form and break rapidly, enabling dynamic molecular interactions
They only form between identical molecules
questions.explanation: The reversibility of hydrogen bonds (~20 kJ/mol) is crucial: DNA strands can separate for replication, proteins can unfold/refold, and enzyme–substrate interactions can be transient yet specific.
